title: SEGMENT 7 — Lister 2009 — the first human methylome at single-base resolution

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Lister 2009 — the first human methylome at single-base resolution
In 2009, Ryan Lister, Mattia Pelizzola, Joe Ecker, and colleagues at the Salk Institute published the paper that opened the human era of methylome research.
The paper, titled "Human DNA methylomes at base resolution show widespread epigenomic differences," appeared in Nature in November 2009.

It reported the first complete, base-resolution methylation maps of two human cell types — human embryonic stem cells (the H1 line) and a fetal fibroblast cell line (IMR90) — using whole-genome bisulfite sequencing on the Illumina platform.
The numbers from that paper give a sense of the scale.
To get adequate coverage, the team sequenced about 87 gigabases of bisulfite-converted DNA per cell line.
They quantified methylation at about 94 percent of the cytosines in the human genome, with at least one read of coverage.
They reported the first comprehensive view of the human methylome at single-base resolution, with quantitative methylation levels for tens of millions of CpG sites.
The paper made several findings that have shaped the field ever since, but two stand out.

First, they confirmed at genome-wide scale what locus-specific studies had hinted at for years: that the human methylome is dominated by methylation at CpG dinucleotides, with the bulk of the genome heavily methylated and CpG islands largely unmethylated.
About 80 percent of CpG sites in IMR90 were methylated, while CpG islands had average methylation below 20 percent.
The expected promoter-methylation rule held up at genome scale.
Second, and more surprisingly, they discovered that human embryonic stem cells carry substantial amounts of methylation at non-CpG contexts — particularly CHG and CHH contexts, where H is A, C, or T.
In differentiated fibroblasts, non-CpG methylation was rare.
In embryonic stem cells, it accounted for a non-trivial fraction of all 5mC and was concentrated in gene bodies.

This was new biology that simply could not have been seen without comprehensive base-resolution methylome data — locus-specific bisulfite Sanger studies would never have stumbled across genome-wide non-CpG methylation, because they were focused on CpG islands by design.
The whole-genome view revealed methylation biology that targeted studies had been blind to.
The Lister 2009 paper became the template for human methylome studies for the next decade.
The Roadmap Epigenomics Project, between 2010 and 2015, used WGBS to map the methylomes of dozens of human tissues and cell types.
The Lister lab and others extended the technique to additional contexts — neurons, induced pluripotent stem cells, cancer cell lines.
The IHEC — International Human Epigenome Consortium — coordinated a global effort to produce reference methylomes for every major human cell type.
By 2020, hundreds of human cell types and tissues had publicly available WGBS profiles, all sitting on the foundation laid by the 2009 Lister paper.
The paper also established the analytical conventions that the field still uses.

Methylation levels are reported as fractions between 0 and 1 — or equivalently as percentages between 0 and 100 — based on the count of C reads divided by the count of C plus T reads at each cytosine position.
Coverage thresholds are set at some minimum number of reads, often 5 to 10, below which methylation calls are considered too noisy to use.
Methylation levels are pooled across CpGs in a region of interest, by some weighted average, to give a region-level methylation summary.
All of these conventions appear in the 2009 paper and have been carried forward, with minor variations, in every methylation pipeline since.
For the rest of this lecture, when I say "WGBS data" or "genome-wide methylation profile," I mean the kind of dataset that the Lister 2009 paper invented.
Base-resolution, quantitative, comprehensive, derived from bisulfite-converted Illumina sequencing.
Hundreds of millions of methylation calls per sample, summarized into a few dozen gigabytes of processed data, ready for downstream analyses like DMR calling, which we'll get to in Segment 9.
